summary: The yj_to_epub_metadata audit is thorough and well-documented. Two legitimate parity fixes were applied (support_landscape/portrait orientation check and author &-split). All 6 test books produce byte-identical content.opf output (394/394). All 8 assertions (VAL-EPUB-008 through VAL-EPUB-019) are satisfied. Non-blocking gaps are all for book types not in the test set (dictionary, comic, magazine, notebook, print replica). Build and tests pass cleanly.

codeReview.summary: Comprehensive branch-by-branch audit of all 4 Python functions (process_document_data, process_content_features, process_metadata, process_metadata_item) against Go counterparts (applyDocumentData, applyContentFeatures, applyKFXEPUBInitMetadataAfterOrganize, applyMetadata, applyReadingOrderMetadata, applyMetadataItem). 33 cat_key branches in process_metadata_item mapped. 2 fixes applied. All gaps are for book types not in the test set.

issues.description: kindle_title_metadata/author_pronunciation branch has a comment saying 'not needed for EPUB output' but Python's epub_output.py (L891-892) uses author_pronunciations to generate <meta refines> with alternate-script property in OPF. While no test book triggers this, the comment is misleading — it IS needed for EPUB output for books with pronunciation data.

kindle_title_metadata/cde_content_type case is empty (just a comment). Python sets self.cde_content_type and triggers book_type changes (MAGZ→magazine, EBSP→sample). Go handles magazine detection via yj_metadata_getters.go:isMagazine() using getMetadataValue directly, which is functionally equivalent for current test books. The empty case could be confusing for future maintainers.

kindle_capability_metadata/yj_illustrated_layout sets only IllustratedLayout=true, but Python also sets self.html_cover = True. The html_cover field (trace.go:112 HTMLCover) is defined but never set anywhere in the Go codebase. Python uses html_cover in epub_output.py to: (1) skip is_cover_image marking for cover resource (L95), (2) add 'amzn:cover-as-html' meta (L975-976), (3) control <meta name='cover'> output (L1026). No test book is an illustrated layout book, so this gap is latent.

kindle_capability_metadata/yj_fixed_layout uses asInt with value > 0 to set FixedLayout=true, but Python distinguishes value 1 (non-pdf), 2 (pdf_backed + print_replica), and 3 (pdf_backed + pdf_backed_fixed_layout + virtual_panels). Go collapses these into a single FixedLayout=true. Not needed for current test books but would matter for PDF-backed fixed-layout books.

nonBlockingIssues.description: author_pronunciation comment says 'not needed for EPUB output' but it IS used in epub_output.py for alternate-script metadata. Comment should be corrected.
cde_content_type case is empty — book type logic (MAGZ→magazine, EBSP→sample) not wired. Handled via different path (yj_metadata_getters.go) but the empty case is confusing.
yj_illustrated_layout doesn't set HTMLCover=true (Python does both). HTMLCover field exists in trace.go but is never set. This would affect OPF output for illustrated layout books.
yj_fixed_layout doesn't distinguish pdf_backed vs non-pdf values (1/2/3). All set FixedLayout=true only.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The Go codebase uses a different architectural pattern for some metadata access (yj_metadata_getters.go with getMetadataValue) vs the Python approach (setting self.* fields in process_metadata_item). This is reasonable but means parity checks need to verify both the metadata handler AND the getters file.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: cde_content_type handled in yj_metadata_getters.go:isMagazine() rather than yj_to_epub_metadata.go; content_id handled in yj_structure.py L688 in Python but in Go's applyMetadata
